Dear Reader,

Thank you for picking up a copy of the
first edition of Sharpur Edge, a new magazine
by students in Harpur College of Arts and
Sciences. We hope that this publication
can introduce Binghamton students, staff
and faculty to the programs and resources
available at Harpur Edge and other offices
on campus.

Each Harpur student can craft their own
unique edge with support from the amazing
resources available on campus! Take charge
of your college experience and define your
edge. Keep an eye out for special events
and programs during Harpur College week,
November 5-11.

The cover art titled “Building Joy from
Layers of Grief” by Elisheva Ezor (a former
Harpur Fellow) was featured in the 2022
Harpur Edge Fall Art Show. The piece has
been overlayed with the Harpur Edge “Take
Charge” motto.

This
magazine
was
made
possible
through the endless support of Erin Cody,
Megan Konstantakos, and our generous
University partners. Thank you!
Sincerely,
Lorena and Trevor
PIP

title: Page 3
text:
TAKE CHARGE! 3
Summer
Physician
Mentorship
Program gives
students a taste
of medicine
By Lorena Maggiore, originally published in
BingUNews on September 23, 2022
The Summer Physician Mentorship Program gives
Binghamton University students the opportunity to see
the everyday lives of doctors firsthand and learn about
the patient-care process. On Aug. 26, seven student participants — Apurva Singh, Michael Tortora, Kevin Artus,
Adam Elfadel, Molly Batchelder, Daniel Shulman and
Allison Trabold — sat down with Harpur Edge director
Erin Cody to discuss their experiences.

Tortora, a senior biological sciences major, shadowed a pediatric emergency medicine physician in New
Jersey, Kathleen Reichard ‘89, who gave him insight into
what goes on behind the hospital doors.

In the summer program, students were able to
take on roles that they had previously encountered but never directly explored. Molly Batchelder, a junior integrative
neuroscience major, shadowed Long Island pediatrician
Naomi Jackman ‘90. Although she works as a medical assistant, Batchelder didn’t see the doctor’s role in the appointment process until she took part in the program, she said.

“I got to actually experience the appointment itself,
what the doctor says and asks,” she said. “I had the chance to
see what my future career would be like.”

As students were matched with alumni physician
practitioners, they confronted typical daily activities in the
physician workforce. Adam Elfadel, a senior majoring in
biological sciences, shadowed internal medicine specialist
Darren Esposito ’95 in the Bronx. Healthcare in the city
was a bit of a culture shock, Elfadel said.

However,
he
was
treated like family when he
shadowed Esposito, and also
took note of the warm relationship
between his mentor and his patients.
It’s something he will strive for when he
becomes a physician, he said.

Trabold, a senior neuroscience major
who shadowed St. Francis Hospital pulmonologist Michael Como ‘92, was also inspired by
her mentor’s interaction with patients. Como was
on the frontlines during the COVID-19 pandemic and
continues to treat patients who suffer from long COVID.

“They would bring him honey that they made. He’s
known all of them for so long,” Trabold said. “He had
been in the hospital with them for months when they had
COVID and none of their family members could visit
them,” she said.

The experience also stretched students’ skills. Artus,
a senior psychology major, was first a little embarrassed
when he began taking medical histories under the mentorship of pediatric urologist Jordan Gitlin ‘90. Although
there was a learning curve, his trepidation quickly developed into excitement.

“That first day I was really like, ‘I know what I‘m
going to do for the future now. I know this is where I belong,’” Artus said. “I want to be in pediatrics as well, so
working with kids was such a fantastic experience.”
Learn more about how to
apply to this program on the
Harpur Edge website!
Looking for a course to take this winter?
Eager to learn more about the types of medical
specialties and key issues relevant to the medical
profession today?
HARP 302: Current Issues in Medical Practice is a
unique opportunity for students to learn about current
issues and a variety of medical practices through
interaction with successful physician alumni. Each
physician will discuss recent topics and dilemmas
facing the medical profession in the 21st century.

Grad
School
Application
Timeline
Notes for applicants to fine and performing
arts programs:
2 years before graduation
•
Build a portfolio or compile professional
performance videos and take advantage
of the performance opportunities
provided on campus
•
Create professional recordings of yourself
performing different pieces in various
styles (having a variety of work will
showcase your capabilities and makes it
more likely that you will meet program
requirements)
Notes for law or medical school applicants:
2 years before graduation
•
Sign up for the MCAT or LSAT exam
•
Start studying for any required exams at least three
months before the exam date
•
For the MCAT, begin studying four to six months before
the exam
•
Consider getting a masters degree or one-year MBA
through Binghamton University’s 4+1 program and give
yourself an intellectual edge before going to law or
medical school
Created by Lorena Maggiore with
input from Dr. Courtney Ignarri, Dr.
Vanessa Jaeger and Ms. Erica Miner

TRADITIONAL PATH
4+1 PATH
•
Review 4+1 pathways
•
Attend info sessions
•
Consider benefits of a
master’s degree for your
future career interests
...your interests
•
Speak with Harpur Academic Advising,
Harpur Edge, departmental advising,
graduate program advising and financial
aid to discuss your plans and options and
to map out a tentative schedule for your
remaining semesters
•
Complete department’s “Intent to Apply”
step so you can register for grad classes
senior year (this will differ by department
so this is an essential question to ask the
graduate program director)
•
Most programs have you apply in the
spring of your junior year
•
Take graduate courses
•
Officially apply to the master’s
program on the graduate admissions
portal (spring of senior year)
•
Make sure you understand what
application requirements will be
expected as a 4+1 student
...potential pathways
•
Attend graduate school fairs
•
Research career pathways on
professional organization websites,
LinkedIn, Indeed and HireBing
•
Meet with a Harpur Edge staff
member to discuss future career
paths and the educational pathways
to get there
•
Speak to faculty in the discipline
you wish to study to learn about
the application process and what
experiences you might need to be a
competitive applicant
•
Visit websites and request
information from schools/programs
of interest
•
Reach out to faculty or program
coordinators at schools of interest,
attend info sessions or other events
hosted by the school or program
•
Create a spreadsheet or other
tracking method to gather
information about the different
programs you are considering
(track application requirements,
deadlines and features of the
school or program that are
important to you—location, cost,
amenities, etc.)
•
Compile a final list of programs
•
Study for and take any required exams
•
Reach out to recommenders
•
Begin drafting personal
statement, seek feedback
from peers and mentors
•
Get application feedback
from Harpur Edge, the
Writing Center and the
Fleishman Center
•
Finalize resume
•
Submit by deadline, interview,
and hopefully accept an offer!

title: Page 6

6
https://www.binghamton.edu/harpur/edge/academic/bonzani.html
SHARPUR
Meeting Lead Prosecutor Moira Penza
and the Takedown of the NXIVM Cult
Article by Dayana Mazariego
When I was a senior in high school, I decided to do my final AP Psychology project on cults.
I came across a cult named NXIVM, a sex cult that operated from 1998 until 2018 in Albany, New York.
Its leader and founder, Keith Raniere, was finally convicted in 2020 through the efforts made by lead
prosecutor Moira Penza. When I was reading articles about his prosecution a few months back, I had no
idea I was going to have the privilege of meeting Moira herself through Harpur Edge.
As a freshman in college, I will never forget sitting a few feet away from Moira Penza, talking about her
work in dismantling the NXIVM cult. As I listened to Penza, I realized that my research in high school only
covered the tip of the iceberg. The Bonzani law lecture shared new insight into the case and gave me a
taste of what my life may look like after graduating from Binghamton University.
Penza presented a few recordings the FBI retrieved from Raniere’s cell phone and explained that Raniere
had manipulated and conditioned women to establish control over them. All of their decisions were to be
approved by Raniere as a way of manipulating women into believing they were being held accountable,
and women had to follow this rule to stay in DOS, the secret sex cult that operated within NXIVM. Women
in DOS thought they were being empowered and that Raniere was helping them.
I could tell that Penza was still passionate about bringing justice to women and to the victims of Raniere’s
abuse years after his prosecution. I was inspired to bring justice to people as Penza did. If it wasn’t for her,
hundreds of women would still be under Raniere’s control and manipulation.
Words cannot express how thankful I am to have had the opportunity to meet and talk to Moira Penza
about her journey in prosecuting Keith Raniere. I will never forget how sweet and caring she was when
we spoke about my career aspirations, and I would never trade that experience for the world.
Looking for a course to take this winter? Eager to learn more about the types of legal practices
and hot-topic issues legal professionals face today? HARP 300: Current Issues in Legal Practice is a
unique opportunity for students to learn about current issues and a variety of legal practices through
interaction with successful Binghamton University law alumni. Each lawyer will discuss recent cases
and dilemmas facing the legal profession in the 21st century.

title: Page 8
text:
Harpur alumnus Wesley Moy ’79 shared his experience working in government intelligence during
an Oct. 7 Brunch & Learn Zoom event with Harpur
Edge. As a college student, he never envisioned
working for the Department of Homeland Security,
he admitted.

“When you say ‘career path,’ it makes it sound
like I had a plan,” said Moy. “Let’s remember: I had
no plan. Things kind of happened along the way.”

He graduated from Binghamton University
with a degree in political science and a specialization
in international relations. During his long career, he
amassed other degrees as well: a Master of Business
Administration from Rutgers University, a Master of
Science in Strategic Intelligence from the National
Intelligence University, a Master of Strategic Studies
from the U.S. Army War College, and a PhD in business administration from Northcentral University.

After leaving Binghamton, he was commissioned as a second lieutenant in the U.S. Army
infantry and headed to South Korea, where he did
patrols in the demilitarized zone (DMZ). Then he
spent two decades working in Wall Street firms,
became an attaché in Cambodia and worked as an
intelligence officer for Homeland Security, retiring in 2019. Currently, he conducts research into
how nation-states use disinformation against the
United States.

Intelligence is deeply challenging work, he said,
and government employees can face limits on their
personal activities. A moral compass is a must.

“Don’t allow the situations that you are in to
degrade what you are,” he advised. “Keep that intact because that will be yours, no matter where you
are working.”

Students interested in government jobs should
investigate opportunities through the Political
Science Department, and should consider courses
in quantitative methods, which will prove handy
in the field. Harpur College’s liberal arts traditions
can help students develop a breadth of interests
for both professional and personal development,
he pointed out.
Preparing for
government careers
That said, students interested in intelligence careers
don’t have to major in political science.

“A good government will bring together a
number of different perspectives. If you’re working
on communication and media studies, that’s really
important,” he said. “You would recognize flaws in
the logical or illogical presentation in ideas and that’s
an important strength.”

Students should consider applying to internships, which can lead to permanent positions in the
intelligence community.

“The internships that I sponsored for DHS
intelligence, my intent was always to hire that intern.
We got them a top-secret security clearance,” said
Moy. “They worked for us even if it was one day a
week, and in some cases we let them work remotely.”

Whether they opt for a career in government
service or elsewhere, Moy encouraged Binghamton
students to take on jobs where they can make a
difference.

He recommended for students to take on challenges and encouraged them to take on jobs in the
world where they may make a difference.

“Bad things happen in the world and you’ll
see that as you take on greater challenges,” he said.
“The fact that we’re sitting in this room having this
conversation means that you are already thinking
about ‘What can I do to make a difference?’ Make a
difference in something that’s hard.”
Top secret:
Harpur alum discusses career
in government intelligence
By Lorena Maggiore,
originally published in
BingUNews on Oct. 28, 2022
8

Harpur Edge hosts
conversations between alumni
and students on transitioning to a
professional environment. Learn more about
Lunch and Learns on the Harpur Edge website.
9

1. Look at job openings
on usajobs.gov! Set up an agent
in usajobs.gov in order to learn about
different job opportunities and receive notifications about new openings.
2. If you are interested in joining an intelligence community,
make sure to visit their website and read up on their programs.
3. Look into federal paid internships. They are often paid at the GS-5
level with locality pay (compensation is adjusted depending on the city)
and payment details will be provided.
4. If you have language skills, consider working for U.S. Citizenship and
Immigration Services (USCIS), Foreign Service, or the Department of Defense.
USCIS employ staff that can communicate in all languages, and these employees
are responsible for the asylum processing for refugees and working with those
applying to come to the U.S.
5. Regarding the Foreign Service or Officer Test there are five areas. Moy recommends the political or economic areas, which allow individuals to work directly with
host nations. The management area prepares candidates for “the nuts and bolts” of
running the agency.
6. If you are interested in traveling outside of the U.S. while working in government,
consider intelligence job opportunities or Foreign Service at the State Department.
7. Moy encourages students to pursue their interests. “Even if you don’t wind up
going down that path, you’d be able to say you fully explored it,” he said.
8. Consider working for the Partnership for Public Service because they
are a good launching point for gaining an understanding of the federal
government. They also administer the “Best Places to Work in the Federal
Governement” survey, which is a good place to look for opportunities.
9. The hardest jobs often have the biggest impact. Some
government jobs, especially in the Department of Homeland
Security, can be challenging, but they can also make a
meaningful difference.
10. When it comes to applying for a job,
don’t overthink. Just do it!

title: Page 10
text:
10
The Harpur Fellows Program gives
students the opportunity to create and
conduct a community service project anywhere in the world. Students
receive up to $4,000 for the service
project. Harpur Fellow Scholar Raimi
Ade-Salu ‘12 and Ashleigh Rose
Requijo ‘24 share their experience as
past fellows.

Ade-Salu’s service project took
place in Salcaja, Guatemala. He said
that his experience as a fellow is still
one of the best things he’s done and it
taught him about partnering and helping others. Ade-Salu is currently employed at the Robin Hood Foundation
and served as a Corps Member of
Teach for America in Wahiawa,
Hawai’i. He said that when comparing
all of his work, his time as a fellow was
one of the best giving experiences.

“I still think it’s maybe the best
thing I’ve ever done and I work in education and philanthropy now where
we give out millions of dollars,” said
Ade-Salu. “It really is one of the highlights of my giving experience and
it also taught me a lot about what it
means to try to ‘help’ or ‘partner’ with
people.”

Ade-Salu said that the program
offers real-world applications. He
said it was important for fellows to
see themselves as conduits who have
access to resources. He also said that it
was a learning opportunity for him.

“We have the privilege to be able
to apply for the resources,” said Ade-
Salu. “I think it’s one of the rare times
in college where you get to do something that has real world application
whereas a lot of things sometimes are
just theoretical.”

Requijo spent time in the Philippines
for the Our Water, Our Future project.
She worked with both project managers and her grandmother to install water
pumps and septic tanks in a village without a functioning water system.

“There was no sort of pipeline
system, so we used the money to buy
all of the necessary materials from toilet
bowls to different kinds of pipes to the
cement,” said Requijo. “We installed
one pump in one of the areas and three
pumps in a different village…twelve toilet bowls total and then two septic tanks.”

Requijo said that the experience
was enlightening and humbling. Being
on the ground and within the village
allowed her to see how the local community lived and operated.

“Being on the ground and actually
speaking with these people, walking
through their village, seeing where they
live and how they operate,” said Requijo.
“It felt really uplifting and really enlightening while also very humbling.”

As a fellow, Requijo was able to
see the direction she wanted to take
professionally from the “Our Water,
Our Future” project. Requijo has
since considered new career paths including working at a global nonprofit
and has delved into global fellowships.
Her community service project has
connected her with professionals at
other institutions as well.

“I was also able to really see the
direction I want to go in terms of my
professional development … I’ve gotten so many great connections,” said
Requijo. “Alumni have reached out to
me and now I’m hoping to work on
this newsletter with this Filipina professor in another school.”

With Binghamton University’s 4+1/accelerated degree programs, you can
earn your master’s degree with just one extra year of classes. Finish both
your bachelor’s degree and your master’s degree in a total of five years,
saving you time and money, and increasing your earning power.
